_comment_purpose: FULL layers.json — every layer in production mapped to a card in the 9-tile IA. Decisions Q1-Q9 + R1-R5 from Session 25 baked in.
_comment_status: Session 25, April 25, 2026. Awaits R2 fill-in (two unaccounted Phase 1 tables) before being final.
_comment_decisions_baked_in: Q2 = a: 5 separate Active Claims cards
Q3 = b: no fetchFn in JSON, JS binding map handles it
Q5 = a: info content from existing LAYER_DESCRIPTIONS
Q6 = a: Custom Hillshade as a card in LiDAR tile
Q7 = a: drag-and-drop tile reorder ported from v6
Q8 = MOOT: no Pro tier, all Pro fields/badges removed
Q9 = MOOT: no anonymous users, localStorage in-session is fine
R1 = a: placer-density renamed to historic-claim-density
R2 = research at port-start (two Phase 1 tables not yet accounted for)
R4 = a: Historical Topos deferred to P2.10
R5 = a: Military Lands source deferred to P1.18 audit

layers.name: Gold Occurrences
layers.desc: USGS-recorded gold sites. Highest-confidence layer to start with.
layers.info: USGS recorded gold sites. Filled dot = confirmed production history. Hollow ring = no recorded production. Yellow = active producer, orange = prospect, grey = occurrence.

layers.name: Historic Mine Sites
layers.desc: Old workings logged in USGS records. Where gold was once produced.
layers.info: USGS recorded mine sites. Orange dot = surface workings. Never enter underground mines — collapse risk.

layers.name: Placer Gold Density
layers.desc: Density of placer gold occurrences. Brighter = more recorded finds.
layers.info: Heat map showing density of historic placer gold claims. Red = historically active area.

layers.name: Lode Gold Density
layers.desc: Density of hardrock workings — quartz veins and fault zones.
layers.info: Heat map showing density of historic lode gold claims. Great for finding hardrock targets.

layers.name: Mercury Occurrences
layers.desc: Found alongside gold in old amalgamation operations. Strong proximity signal.
layers.info: USGS recorded mercury sites. Mercury and gold often deposit together in hydrothermal systems — mercury presence nearby is a prospecting indicator.

layers.name: Chromium Occurrences
layers.desc: Often points to ultramafic rock — host for nickel-copper-PGE, sometimes gold.
layers.info: USGS recorded chromium sites. Chromium forms in ultramafic rocks (serpentinite) that host gold in Oregon and California districts. Strong indicator of gold-bearing geology.

layers.name: Copper Occurrences
layers.desc: Common gold pathfinder in volcanic and porphyry settings.
layers.info: USGS recorded copper sites. Copper and gold are common companions in porphyry and skarn deposits — high copper density can indicate gold-bearing hydrothermal systems.

layers.name: Antimony Occurrences
layers.desc: Gold pathfinder, especially in epithermal vein systems.
layers.info: USGS recorded antimony sites. Antimony (stibnite) is a key pathfinder mineral for gold — many major gold deposits worldwide have antimony anomalies nearby.

layers.name: Silver Occurrences
layers.desc: Frequently mineralized with gold; closer in low-sulfidation epithermal deposits.
layers.info: USGS recorded silver sites. Silver and gold are frequently co-deposited in epithermal veins. Silver-rich areas are strong indicators of precious metal mineralization.

layers._comment: NGDB Coming Soon — sub-section threshold met when wired
layers.name: Rock Geochemistry
layers.desc: USGS rock-sample geochemistry. 819K data points across the western US.
layers.info: USGS National Geochemical Database — rock samples assayed for major and trace elements. Useful for identifying anomalous mineralization zones.

layers.name: Sediment Geochemistry
layers.desc: USGS stream-sediment chemistry. Strongest single dataset for area-scale prospecting.
layers.info: USGS National Geochemical Database — stream sediment samples. The ratio of trace elements in sediment indicates upstream mineralization.

layers.name: Soil Geochemistry
layers.desc: USGS soil chemistry. Surface-level mineral signatures.
layers.info: USGS National Geochemical Database — soil samples. Soil geochemistry reflects what's eroding out of the bedrock above.

layers.name: Concentrate Geochemistry
layers.desc: USGS heavy-mineral concentrate chemistry. Direct prospector-relevant data.
layers.info: USGS National Geochemical Database — heavy-mineral concentrate samples. Concentrates are what prospectors actually pan for; this is the closest historic analog to a prospector's results.

layers.name: Quaternary Faults
layers.desc: Active fault traces. Hydrothermal fluids carry gold up through fractures.
layers.info: USGS Quaternary faults — bedrock fractures that have moved in the last 2.6 million years. Faults are natural conduits for hydrothermal fluids that carry gold up from depth, so they frequently mark the edges of gold-bearing districts. Prospect near the fault trace, not directly on it — look for crushed rock, quartz veining, and iron staining within a quarter mile of the line.

layers.name: All Active Claims
layers.desc: All currently-active BLM claims — placer, lode, mill, and tunnel combined.
layers.info: All BLM mining claims currently active in this region. Green outlines on the map.

layers.name: Active Placer Claims
layers.desc: Placer claims only. Surface deposits, generally streams and gravels.
layers.info: Active placer claims only — gold in stream gravels. Best for panning and detecting.

layers.name: Active Lode Claims
layers.desc: Lode claims only. Hardrock veins and bedrock-hosted gold.
layers.info: Active lode claims only — hardrock gold in quartz veins. Requires drilling or blasting.

layers.name: Active Tunnel Claims
layers.desc: Underground access claims for hardrock workings.
layers.info: Active tunnel site claims — access tunnels to underground workings.

layers.name: Active Mill Claims
layers.desc: Mill site claims for ore processing operations.
layers.info: Active mill site claims — land used for ore processing, not actual mining.

layers.name: Closed / Lapsed Claims
layers.desc: Recently expired claims. Ground was worth staking once, now open again.
layers.info: Historical claims that have lapsed or been abandoned. Strong indicator of past gold finds.

layers._comment: R1: renamed from placer-density. Production code references must update during port.
layers.name: Historic Claim Density
layers.desc: Where claims were heavily clustered historically. High-interest ground for relocation work.
layers.info: Density of all historical claims combined. Red zones had the most mining activity.

layers.name: Surface Mgmt Agencies
layers.desc: Who controls the surface — BLM, USFS, NPS, etc.
layers.info: Federal land surface management — BLM, USFS, NPS, FWS shown by agency.

layers.name: Open to Claim (BLM)
layers.desc: Federal land where mining claims are legally permitted.
layers.info: BLM land currently open for mineral entry under the 1872 Mining Law. Always verify locally.

layers.name: PLSS Survey Grid
layers.desc: Township-Range-Section grid. Required for staking claim corners legally.
layers.info: Public Land Survey System grid — the township/range grid used to describe claim locations.

layers.name: BLM Easements (PLAD)
layers.desc: BLM-recorded public access easements across private land.
layers.info: BLM Public Land Access Database — easement lines that grant public access through otherwise private land. 4,675 features landed; awaiting tileset publish.

layers.name: BLM Reservations (PLAD)
layers.desc: BLM access reservations as polygons.
layers.info: BLM Public Land Access Database — reservation polygons. 89 features landed; awaiting tileset publish.

layers.name: BLM Roads & Trails
layers.desc: Drivable BLM road network for accessing claim areas.
layers.info: BLM maintained roads and trails. Shows access routes into backcountry mining areas.

layers.name: USFS Roads (MVUM)
layers.desc: Forest Service Motor Vehicle Use Map — road network.
layers.info: USFS Motor Vehicle Use Map roads layer. Defines what's drivable in National Forests. 150K features landed; awaiting tileset publish.

layers.name: USFS Trails (MVUM)
layers.desc: Forest Service Motor Vehicle Use Map — trail network.
layers.info: USFS Motor Vehicle Use Map trails layer. Single-track and ATV-grade routes. 28K features landed; awaiting tileset publish.

layers.name: BLM GTLF — Motorized
layers.desc: BLM Ground Transportation Linear Features — motorized routes.
layers.info: BLM Ground Transportation Linear Features (Public Display). Motorized vehicle routes on BLM-managed land. 108K features landed; awaiting tileset publish.

layers.name: BLM GTLF — Limited Use
layers.desc: BLM ground transportation routes with usage restrictions.
layers.info: BLM GTLF restricted-use routes — seasonal closures, permit requirements, etc. 25K features landed; awaiting tileset publish.

layers.name: National Parks
layers.desc: No claiming, no extraction. Mark to avoid.
layers.info: No prospecting of any kind permitted under federal law.

layers.name: Wilderness Areas
layers.desc: Wilderness Act lands. Closed to mineral entry.
layers.info: No motorized equipment. Hand panning may be allowed — verify with local ranger district.

layers.name: National Monuments
layers.desc: Most monuments are withdrawn from mineral entry — confirm per-monument.
layers.info: New mining claims prohibited. Existing valid claims may continue.

layers.name: Wild & Scenic Rivers
layers.desc: Protected river corridors with quarter-mile buffers on each bank.
layers.info: Motorized dredging restricted. Hand panning may be allowed — check with ranger district.

layers.name: Tribal Lands
layers.desc: Reservation lands. Sovereign jurisdiction — separate permitting required.
layers.info: Sovereign tribal land. No access without tribal permission. Federal law applies.

layers._comment: R5 deferred — ArcGIS REST source kept for now. Migration to landed military_lands table tracked under P1.18.
layers.name: Military Areas
layers.desc: Active and former military boundaries — generally restricted access.
layers.info: Military installation. No prospecting, detecting, or unauthorized entry.

layers.name: Stream Gauges (Live)
layers.desc: Real-time USGS stream flow. Tells you if a creek is runnable today.
layers.info: Live USGS water flow data. Green = low water ideal for panning. Red = flood conditions, stay out.

layers.name: Standard Gray Hillshade
layers.desc: Industry-standard hillshade. Reveals old workings, ditches, tailings.
layers.info: Classic single-angle shaded relief. The industry-standard hillshade — best balance of detail and clarity for most terrain.

layers.name: Multidirectional
layers.desc: Multi-angle lighting. Best for ambiguous slopes.
layers.info: Lit from four sun angles combined. Reveals subtle features from multiple directions — good for ridges and ravines.

layers.name: Elevation Tinted
layers.desc: Hillshade plus elevation color — quick read for high-vs-low ground.
layers.info: Hillshade blended with elevation colors (green low, brown high). Shows terrain relief and altitude at a glance.

layers.name: Gray-Stretch
layers.desc: Contrast-stretched gray. Boosts faint relief in flat country.
layers.info: Gray hillshade with contrast stretched. Boosts faint relief in flat country — good for benches, terraces, and faded old workings.

layers.name: Low Angle
layers.desc: Sun at 15°. Long shadows exaggerate micro-relief.
layers.info: Sun at 15° altitude. Long shadows exaggerate subtle relief — best for spotting old adits, cuts, and tailings piles.

layers.name: East-Lit
layers.desc: Sun from the east. Catches features oriented north-to-south.
layers.info: Sun from the east. Highlights north-south features that standard hillshade tends to flatten.

layers.name: South-Lit
layers.desc: Sun from the south. Catches features oriented east-to-west.
layers.info: Sun from the south. Highlights east-west features — good for reading stream terraces and cross-valley structures.

layers.name: Slope Map
layers.desc: Color-coded by steepness. Red = steep cliffs, blue = flat ground.
layers.info: Colored by steepness. Red = steep cliffs, blue = flat ground. Pinpoints benches and break-in-slope locations.

layers.name: Aspect Map
layers.desc: Color-coded by compass direction the slope faces.
layers.info: Colored by the compass direction slopes face. Useful for identifying consistent hillside orientations and weather exposure.

layers.name: Contour Smoothed 25
layers.desc: Smoothed 25-ft contour lines. Layers cleanly on top of any base.
layers.info: Vector elevation lines at smoothed 25-ft intervals. Overlay on any hillshade to read elevation numerically.

layers._comment: Q6 = a: Custom Hillshade as a card. Replaces standalone panel near zoom indicator.
layers.name: Custom Hillshade
layers.desc: Build your own. Adjust sun direction, sun angle, vertical exaggeration.
layers.info: Build your own hillshade. Adjust sun direction, sun angle, and vertical exaggeration to reveal features others miss.

layers.name: 3D Terrain
layers.desc: Tilt the map to see the landscape in 3D.
layers.info: Raises the map into a 3D landscape. Tilt to see valley and ridge shapes.

layers.name: Contour Lines
layers.desc: Standard topographic contours for elevation reading.
layers.info: Elevation lines at regular intervals. Closer lines = steeper ground.
